title: Chapter Thirty-Four: The Witness
content: The wood of the witness box is old. I can see it from the gallery — the worn grain, the dark ring where someone's hand has gripped the railing a thousand times, the indentation near the microphone where a heel or a ring or a fist has marked it. This is not a place built for comfort. It is built for extraction.
Margaret Grose walks to it.

She walks slowly, which is not performative. She is sixty-three and her left knee has been bad since winter and the steps are steep. The usher holds the gate and she nods, once, and takes her seat. The cushion is thin. She places both hands flat on the railing — the same railing with that worn-dark ring — and looks out at the courtroom.

She does not look at the journalists packed three deep along the back wall, their laptops balanced on their knees, fingers already moving. She does not look at the public gallery, where I sit between a woman from the BBC and a man who takes notes on index cards with a pencil. She does not look at the empty dock — Henry's attendance waived until the close of the prosecution case.
She looks at Sarah Chen.

Sarah stands at the lectern in the dark suit with the small fray at the left cuff. Her file is open but she's not looking at it. She's looking at Mrs. Grose with an expression I cannot parse — not warmth, not professional detachment. Something earned.
The court officer asks Mrs. Grose to swear the oath. She places her hand on the book — worn leather, gold lettering mostly gone — and says the words. Her voice is steady. It will not stay steady.
"Mrs. Grose, can you state your name for the record?"
"Margaret Anne Grose."
"And your occupation?"
"I was the housekeeper at Bly Manor for twenty-two years. I resigned in September of this year."

"Mrs. Grose, I'm going to take you through a series of events. Some may incriminate you. You've been advised of your right against self-incrimination and you've chosen to testify regardless. Is that correct?"
"Yes."
"Can you tell the court, in your own words, when you first became aware of what has been referred to in these proceedings as 'the Game'?"

Mrs. Grose's hands tighten on the railing. The courtroom is so quiet I can hear the building's heating system, warm air pushing through old ducts. I can smell the institutional smell of this place — floor polish, old paper, the faint chemical sweetness of the microphone sanitizer. And underneath: bound legal texts, leather and dust and the slow decay of cellulose, lining the walls in shelves that reach the ceiling. History preserved. History compounding.

"Peter arrived at Bly in the summer of 2008," Mrs. Grose says. "After his parents died. He was ten. Henry was his guardian. I'd been running the house for six years by then. Peter was..." She pauses. "He was a difficult child. Not unkind. Damaged. And Henry was patient with him in a way that, looking back, I should have questioned."
"In what way should you have questioned it?"

"Henry let Peter do things. Push boundaries. Test people. He called it 'building resilience.' Peter would play games with the staff — not normal children's games. Psychological ones. He'd learn things about them, their fears, their weaknesses, and use those things. And Henry would watch. Sometimes encourage it."
"The first Game occurred in 2008. Can you describe it?"

"A girl named Sarah Poole was hired as a temporary governess. She lasted three weeks. Peter tormented her — systematically. He found out about her eating disorder. He left food in strategic places. He made comments. He monitored her and reported to Henry like a—" She stops. Swallows. "Like a research assistant. That was the first Game. Seeing how long it would take to break someone. Sarah Poole left in the middle of the night. She just ran."
The journalists are typing. The sound is like rainfall — a soft clicking that underscores everything.
"The second governess was Claire Hargreaves, 2010. She didn't run."

"She broke differently. She stayed. Peter isolated her from her family. Intercepted her letters. Convinced her that her friends didn't care, that her family was relieved she was gone. And then, when she had nothing left outside Bly, he withdrew. Pretended it had all been a game. She had a breakdown in 2011. Hospitalised for three months. Henry paid for the treatment. She signed a non-disclosure agreement."
"Mrs. Grose, did you witness this?"
"I witnessed all of it."
"Did you report it?"
"No."
"Why not?"
The silence that follows is not theatrical. It is the silence of a woman who has asked herself that question every day for fifteen years and never found an answer that satisfies.

"I was afraid," she says finally. "Henry controlled everything. The house, the money, my employer's reference, my visa status — I'm not a British citizen, Mrs. Chen. I came from Jamaica on a work permit he sponsored. If I lost that position, I lost my right to be in this country. And I told myself—" Her voice cracks for the first time. "I told myself it wasn't that bad. I told myself the girls were fragile. I told myself Peter was just a child. I told myself whatever I needed to tell myself to keep making bread in that kitchen every morning."
The judge shifts. I hear the creak of his chair, the whisper of his robes. The courtroom clock ticks — analog, white face, black hands, the kind that makes you hear every second pass.
Sarah gives her a moment. Not pity. Respect.
"The third governess was Helen Achebe. 2013."
"Helen was different. Older. Teaching certificate, a career. Peter couldn't get to her the same way. So Henry intervened directly."
"What do you mean?"

"Henry told Helen that Peter had a degenerative neurological condition. He said Peter's behaviour was a symptom. He asked Helen to participate in a therapeutic protocol — structured activities, psychological exercises. He made the Game sound like medicine. She believed him. Participated for eight months. When she found out the truth — no illness, she'd been part of an experiment — she confronted Henry. And then she disappeared."
"Disappeared?"

"Henry said he'd relocated her. Found her another position, far from Bly. Said it was for her own good. I asked where she'd gone. He said it wasn't my concern. I never saw Helen again. No forwarding address. No reference. She was just... gone."
"Did you believe Henry's explanation at the time?"
"I wanted to believe it."
"Do you believe it now?"
"No."
The word hangs in the air. I can taste something — the dryness of my own mouth, the staleness of recycled heat.
Sarah moves to the fourth governess. Jessel. I know what's coming and I still feel the air change when Mrs. Grose says her name.

"Miss Jessel arrived in 2015. She was twenty-four. She thought she could handle it. She was wrong. Peter had refined his methods by then. And he had help. Miles was eight years old and already..." She searches for the word. "Already recruited. Miles would watch Peter, learn from him, mimic him. Two players instead of one."
"Can you describe the events of the night of August the fourteenth, 2015?"
Mrs. Grose's hands are shaking now. Not trembling — shaking. Both of them, visible even from the gallery.

"Peter told Miss Jessel the Game had a final round. If she completed it, she'd be free — he'd leave her alone permanently. The final round was to walk into the lake. Not swim. Walk in, up to her shoulders, stay for thirty seconds. He timed it on his phone. Miles was on the shore, filming."
"Did you see this?"

"I arrived halfway through. I heard Peter's voice, excited, counting down. I went to the lake and saw Miss Jessel in the water, up to her neck, smiling — this terrible smile, like she was proving something. And then she went under."
"How long was she under?"
"A few seconds. She came back up, coughing, laughing — this broken, frightened laugh. And then she went under again and she didn't come up."
"Did you attempt to help her?"

"I walked into the lake. I did. Up to my knees. The water was cold — August but it's always cold, fed from underground springs. The bottom was mud pulling at my feet. The water was over my waist and I can't swim. I never learned. I grew up inland, in the mountains. She was further out and she was struggling and I couldn't—"
She stops. The courtroom is silent except for the clock and the heating and the soft clatter of a journalist's laptop being closed, carefully, so as not to make a sound.

"I couldn't reach her. I stood there, in the water, up to my chest. I watched her go under for the last time. And then I watched the surface. And then there was nothing. Just the lake. The ripples spreading out and then stopping."
"What happened then?"

"I walked back to shore. Soaked. Shaking. And I saw Miles put his phone away. He was ten years old. He looked at me — looked right at me — and walked back to the house. He didn't say anything. He just walked."
"And Peter?"
"Peter was already gone. He'd run back the moment she went under. He was in his room by the time I got back. I could hear him through the door." Her voice goes flat. "He was laughing."

Mrs. Grose is not crying. I don't know why that's worse. It's as though whatever part of her produces tears was removed long ago, scooped out like the core of a spoiled fruit, and what's left is just the shell.
"What did you do then, Mrs. Grose?"
"I went to the kitchen. Took off my wet clothes. Put on my spare uniform. Started making bread. I kneaded the dough and I put it in the oven and I never told anyone."
"Did Henry know what had happened?"

"Henry arrived home the following morning. He spoke to Peter. Then to me. He told me what to say to the police. Miss Jessel had left the residence two days prior. I had not seen her since. I said those things to the police. Both times they came. I said those exact things."
"Why did you lie, Mrs. Grose?"

"Because Henry told me to. Because I was afraid. Because the bread was rising in the oven and I had to take it out or it would burn and it seemed like such a small thing, a loaf of bread, but it was the only thing I could control. So I controlled it. I took the bread out. I let the lie rise instead."

The cross-examination is brutal. Henry's QC is a woman named Davies — silver hair, surgical voice, controlled aggression that doesn't require volume. She rises and buttons her jacket and approaches the witness box like someone approaching a carcass with a scalpel.

"Mrs. Grose. You admit you stood by while a woman drowned. You admit you lied to the police — twice. You admit you destroyed evidence — your wet clothing, and any other physical traces of your presence at the lake. You admit you facilitated a cover-up that lasted eight years. And you continued to work for Henry Bly, cooking his meals, cleaning his home, for eight more years after a woman died under his roof." She lets each item land. "Why should this court believe anything you say?"
Mrs. Grose looks at her. Looks at the judge. Looks out at the courtroom — the journalists, the cameras, the wooden panels, the clock. She looks at all of it and then she looks down at her own hands.
"Because I'm the only one left who was there. And because I'd rather go to prison for telling the truth than live another day with the lie."

Davies has follow-up questions. Many of them. The judge allows them, one after another, methodical and grinding. But something has shifted. That answer — plain, unadorned, un-lawyered — has done something no cross-examination can undo. It has told the truth about the truth. It has said: this is not redemption. This is not absolution. This is just what's left when you stop running.

The cross-examination ends at four-seventeen. Mrs. Grose is excused. She steps down from the witness box, and I watch her walk out the side door, and I am already moving before I know I've decided to.

The corridor is cream-painted and fluorescent-lit and smells of floor cleaner. There is a bench against the wall — wooden, dark, the varnish worn through in the middle where thousands of people have sat and waited and stared at nothing. Mrs. Grose is sitting on it. She is shaking.

Not crying. Shaking. A continuous tremor through her shoulders and down her arms and into her hands, which are folded in her lap, interlocked, holding each other. Her eyes are fixed on the opposite wall where a fire exit sign glows red.

I sit next to her. I don't speak. The bench is cold through my clothes — the kind of cold that leaches up from the stone floor, that sits in the wood, that lives in this building built from Edinburgh granite. The corridor hums with distant sound: a door closing somewhere, footsteps on the floor above, the muffled voice of a clerk.
Two minutes. Three. Four. The shaking doesn't stop.

"I thought it would feel different," Mrs. Grose says. Her voice is quiet, almost conversational, which makes it worse. "Telling the truth. I thought it would feel like something. Lighter. Cleaner. It doesn't feel different. It just feels empty."

I look at her. The fluorescent light flattens her face, emphasizing the lines, the shadows under her eyes, the texture of skin that has spent two decades near ovens and steam and flour. She looks exactly like what she is: a tired woman who has done a necessary thing that cost her everything and gave her nothing.
"Maybe empty is what honest feels like," I say.
She turns her head. Her expression is unreadable. Not grateful. Not angry. Just present.
"Aye," she says. "Maybe it is."
She goes back to staring at the wall. The shaking slows, eventually. It doesn't stop.
My phone vibrates at five-forty. I'm in the lobby, holding a cup of coffee I don't want, watching the last journalists leave. The text is from Sarah.
*Henry's been arrested. Not for the Game — obstruction of justice, perverting the course of justice, conspiracy to conceal a death. The Crown has enough now.*

I stare at the words. I wait for something to move in me — triumph, relief, satisfaction. Nothing comes. Just the same emptiness Mrs. Grose described, sitting in this lobby that smells of old carpet and vending-machine coffee and the ghost of today's newspapers.
A second text.
*Miles is sixteen. In the UK he could be tried as an adult for assisting a death. His lawyer wants to deal.*
A third.
*He says Miles has information about Helen — the third governess. The one who was "relocated."*

Relocated. The word sits on my screen, inert and flat. Not to another position. Not to another house. I think of Mrs. Grose's testimony, the way she said *gone* — the way she pressed her lips together, as though the word had a taste she didn't like.
Relocated where?
I type back: *What does Miles know about Helen that even Mrs. Grose doesn't?*
The three dots appear. Disappear. Appear again. Sarah is thinking about how to say what she's about to say, which means it's bad, which means whatever answer exists is one I'm not ready for.
*Miles says Helen didn't leave Bly. He says he knows where she is.*

Where. Not where she went. Where she *is*. Present tense. A woman who disappeared in 2013, who has been gone for thirteen years — a woman whose absence Mrs. Grose described with the flat finality of a headstone.
Miles knows where she is.

I set down the coffee. I look at the lobby doors, where the last of the daylight is fading, and I think: this is what justice feels like. Not closure. Not resolution. Just the opening of another door behind which something waits, patient and dark and older than the question I just learned to ask.
What happened to Helen Achebe?
The clock above the lobby desk ticks. The door swings shut. The heat clicks on.
I'm still sitting here.
